source.definition: A tender, informal way to express affection and love towards someone, similar to 'I love you' but often softer and more intimate.

explanation.sourceText: The phrase 'I love you' uses the English verb 'love', which comes from Old English 'lufu', meaning affection or belovedness.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „love” provine din engleza veche „lufu”, care însemna afecțiune sau dragoste.

target.definition: O expresie informală și afectuoasă pentru a exprima dragostea față de cineva, echivalentă cu 'Ich hab dich lieb' im Deutschen, aber direkter als 'Te iubesc'.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb 'a iubi' derives from the Latin 'iubēre', which originally meant 'to command' but evolved in Romanian to mean 'to love'.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a iubi” provine din latinul „iubēre”, care inițial însemna „a porunci”, dar în română a evoluat cu sensul de „a iubi”.

target.additional: Verb of the 3rd conjugation: eu iubesc, tu iubești, el/ea iubește.

examples.sourceText: I love you, even when we argue.
examples.targetText: Te iubesc, chiar dacă ne certăm.

examples.sourceText: Tell your mother that you love her.
examples.targetText: Spune-i mamei tale că o iubești.

examples.sourceText: Children often say 'I love you' to their parents.
examples.targetText: Copiii spun adesea „Te iubesc” părinților lor.

learningTips.text: Remember 'Te iubesc' sounds like 'tea you-besk'—imagine sharing tea with someone you love to recall the phrase.
The Romanian verb 'a iubi' comes from Latin 'iubēre', originally meaning 'to command'—think of love as a heartfelt command to care.
Link the English phrase 'I love you' with 'Te iubesc' by focusing on the shared feeling of affection despite different sounds.
Picture saying 'I love you' softly to someone and hearing 'Te iubesc' back—both express love, but 'Te iubesc' is more direct.
